items.correct: Producing a specific quantity of identical items, then switching to make another batch.
items.why: Students confuse batch with mass production. Batch produces FIXED QUANTITIES with the line then changing - between one-off (single item) and mass (continuous high volume).
Students use magnetism as the test, but stainless steel (ferrous) can be non-magnetic. The defining property is iron content - 'ferrous' comes from the Latin for iron.

items.q: Which of the 6 Rs has the GREATEST environmental benefit?
items.correct: Refuse or Reduce (preventing the impact at source).
items.why: Students treat recycling as the gold standard, but recycling uses energy and downgrades materials. The hierarchy is Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Repair, Recycle, Rethink - PREVENTION beats reprocessing.

items.q: Which ISO standard governs Life Cycle Assessment principles?
items.why: Correct answer: ISO 14040. “ISO 9001” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Whose 2002 Cradle to Cradle inspired circular design?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: McDonough and Braungart. “Manzini and Vezzoli” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximately what embodied energy does aluminium have (MJ per kg)?
items.why: The real figure is 155 MJ/kg. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
